title: Introduction
text: You've already mastered the Python GDS workflow using the Movies dataset. Now, it's time to take those skills and apply them to a real-world research problem: analyzing academic citation networks.

title: What You'll Learn
text: By the end of this lesson, you'll have the ability to explain what citation networks reveal about scientific research. You'll know how to load the Cora citation dataset into Neo4j, create a graph projection configured for citation analysis, and describe the structure of the Cora dataset.

title: From Movies to Research
text: In the previous lessons, you learned how to connect to Neo4j with the Python client, create graph projections with properties, run algorithms in different modes, and chain algorithms together. Now, you'll be applying these skills to a new domain.

title: The Citation Network Problem
text:
Academic research builds on previous work, and citation networks can reveal some fascinating insights. They help us identify influential papers that have shaped entire fields, bridge papers that connect different research areas, and research communities that form natural clusters.

[IMAGE: img-3-python-client-5-citation-network-setup-s436-001-new]
As you can see here, the diagram illustrates a citation network with nodes representing academic papers and edges representing citations. Notice how the highlighted nodes indicate influential papers, bridge papers, and community papers, each with distinct colors.

title: The Cora Dataset
text: The Cora dataset is a classic benchmark for graph machine learning. It includes 2,708 academic papers, 10,556 citation relationships, and spans across 7 subject areas.

title: Subject Areas
text: The subject areas covered in the Cora dataset include Neural Networks, Reinforcement Learning, Theory, Genetic Algorithms, Case-Based Reasoning, Probabilistic Methods, and Rule Learning.

title: Graph Structure
text:
Let's dive into the graph structure of our citation network. We have nodes labeled as 'Paper' with properties like 'subject', 'features', and 'subjectClass'. The relationships are directed, with 'CITES' indicating that Paper A cites Paper B.

[IMAGE: img-3-python-client-5-citation-network-setup-s439-003-new]
Here, the diagram details the graph structure, showing nodes as papers with properties and directed edges as citation relationships. Each paper is represented by a 1,433-dimensional vector that captures the words used in it.

title: What You'll Accomplish
text:
In the upcoming notebooks, you'll load the Cora citation dataset, project the citation network into GDS, run PageRank to find influential papers, run Betweenness Centrality to identify bridge papers, detect communities to find research clusters, and combine these results for a comprehensive analysis.

[IMAGE: img-3-python-client-5-citation-network-setup-s440-004-new]
This flowchart illustrates the steps in analyzing a citation network, from loading data to running algorithms and combining results.

title: Loading the Dataset
text: Let's start by loading the paper nodes. We'll use a Cypher query to load the data from a CSV file, merging each row as a 'Paper' node with its properties. Once executed, you'll see a confirmation of how many papers were loaded.

title: Loading Relationships
text: Next, we'll load the citation relationships. Using another Cypher query, we'll match source and target papers and merge them with a 'CITES' relationship. After running this, you'll get a count of how many citations were successfully loaded.

title: Verify Your Data
text: After loading, it's important to verify the dataset. We'll run a query to check the distribution of papers by subject. You should see seven subjects with papers distributed across them.

title: Node and Relationship Counts
text: Let's verify the counts of nodes and relationships. By running a Cypher query, you should see a total of 2,708 papers and 10,556 citations. This confirms that our data is loaded correctly.

title: Creating the Projection
text: Now, we'll project the citation network. Using the GDS library, we'll create a graph projection named 'cora-graph', specifying 'Paper' nodes with 'subjectClass' properties and 'CITES' relationships as undirected.

title: Why UNDIRECTED?
text:
Even though citations are directional, for influence analysis, we treat them as connections in both directions. This approach captures the network of related research rather than just the influence flow.

[IMAGE: img-3-python-client-5-citation-network-setup-s446-010-new]
The diagram here compares directed and undirected graphs, illustrating why citations are treated as undirected for influence analysis.

title: Inspecting the Projection
text: Let's inspect the details of our projected graph. We'll print out its name, the number of nodes and relationships, memory usage, and density. This gives us a clear picture of our graph's structure.

title: Summary
text: You're now ready to analyze citation networks. With a dataset of 2,708 papers, 10,556 citations, and 7 research areas, your goal is to find influential papers, bridge papers, and research communities. You'll use the same Python GDS skills you've already learned. Next, we'll run PageRank to find those influential papers.
